story:
The day I battled my first dragon began like any other. Sun warmed my nose, but my body stayed tight: toes curled, hair up, jaw clenched from a too-real dream of night hunts. I cracked one eye—no feathers, no fur, just my room. Father snored beside me; Mother’s side was warm and empty. I slid into her spot, even though I’m not supposed to, when a clatter and a sharp cry split the air.

Open, you damned door! I ordered the barrier. Sounds and scents seeped under it—Mother rushing, the Youngling’s tears, the bitter bite of coffee. Something was wrong. Open! I tried again. Father only snorted. I braced to ram it when a click—Mother appeared with the Youngling, her cheeks wet. I wagged and sniffed, wanting to fix whatever hurt, but Mother pushed me into the living room. “Beowulf, now!”

The Youngling sprang on me, arms tight around my neck. Release me, I commanded, licking her sticky cheeks until she giggled free. Pancakes. I tried to help Mother clean crumbs, tongue at the ready, but her forearm blocked me. “Not for you!” She didn’t even give me my usual kiss. What is wrong, Mother?

Behind me the Youngling upended a box, the floor clinking with a hundred things. “That’s it! Outside! Both of you!” Mother’s voice made my tail tuck. I wanted to stay and guard her, but I obeyed, ears down, out the door with the Youngling.

The yard washed over me: sun, grass, the path I’ve worn along the fence. I patrolled, then marked the oak. Branches shook. Intruder! A blue bird eyed me. I clawed at the trunk. Intruder! It fled. My house.

Satisfied, I checked on the Youngling chalking the patio. She squealed when I stepped across her colors, so I backed off. I am not a puppy anymore. I have responsibilities. Nose low, I inspected ants, snails, roly-polies, all tempting, but duty called.

Between two flower pots, my whiskers twitched—new. A sudden scream. “Get it, Woofie, get it!” The Youngling jabbed a finger. I searched—nothing but sun-warmed concrete and ants—until I saw it: motionless, shimmering green-black-white scales, unblinking bead eyes, a pink cave of a mouth hissing. A dragon.

Intruder! Intruder! I warned. It did not flinch. My belly tightened. I did not know how to fight such a thing. I pressed my nose to the Youngling’s leg. I protect, Youngling. For her, I stepped forward, one paw, then another. The dragon scuttled closer, a stutter-step across stone. No creature had ever defied me like this. I bared my teeth. Leave! Now!

Pain exploded across my nose. The beast clamped down. I yelped, shook, swatted. Mother’s voice cut the air; a broom whooshed, and with a flick the dragon skittered into the bushes, its severed tail still thrashing on the stones.

Victory! I proclaimed, eyeing the defeated piece. “Shhh, it’s OK,” Mother told the Youngling, then cupped my jaw, checking my stinging nose. “Looks OK.” Father arrived. “Did you protect our little princess? Good boy!” He rubbed my belly. I’d hoped for more for such bravery, but Father is busy.

The day smoothed back into ordinary. Father ate. Mother’s edge softened. The Youngling stayed inside. I climbed the rumpled bed, curled in the last warm sun patch, and drifted off, nose throbbing, hoping for dreams of matted gray fur, fluttering feathers—or pancakes.
